skill.name: firefox-browser
skill.description: Control a user-approved Firefox session through a browser bridge for navigation, page inspection, form entry, screenshots, and authenticated workflows.
skill.summary: Use a real Firefox session to inspect pages, interact with forms, and manage authenticated browser workflows.
skill.author: 1jehuang

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported many external command and weak-crypto findings because Markdown command examples and backticked action names were treated as executable Ruby. No prompt injection or hidden exfiltration intent was found, but the skill intentionally controls a logged-in Firefox session, captures page content, and can take screenshots, so publication should include a clear sensitive-data warning.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Browser Session Access
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to control the user's real Firefox session with existing logins and cookies. This can expose private pages and enable actions as the user if prompts are not reviewed carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The description explicitly says it uses the user's real browser with existing logins and cookies. The authentication section confirms the bridge leverages existing browser sessions.

medium_findings.title: Potentially Sensitive Page Capture
medium_findings.description: The skill can read page content, list interactable elements, and capture visible browser screenshots. These features are legitimate for browser automation but may reveal account data or private page content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The action reference directly documents getContent, getInteractables, and screenshot features. No upload destination is shown, so the risk is sensitive capture rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Visible Browser Action Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill can click, type, fill forms, navigate pages, and manage authentication prompts. These actions can change account state or submit data on authenticated websites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented actions include click, type, fillForm, and authentication controls. This confirms a real capability to interact with user accounts, although no malicious target is present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external-command alerts are Markdown examples for the browser CLI, not hidden executable code inside a script. They still show that the skill depends on shell access and a local browser command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced bash examples and troubleshooting instructions. They are operational instructions, not code that executes automatically when the skill is loaded.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Example Navigation Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are examples such as example.com, amazon.com, and walmart.com. They demonstrate navigation behavior and are not shown receiving secrets or screenshots.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear inside usage examples for starting sessions and comparing prices. No evidence found that these URLs are exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: No Evidence for Weak Cryptography Finding
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts appear to match Markdown table separators, action names, or formatting. No cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas are action-reference tables, not crypto code. Because only SKILL.md exists in the scanned file set, there is no additional code evidence for this alert.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Browser Bridge Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to run browser CLI commands through Bash. This is expected for the bridge but increases risk if command arguments are built from untrusted page text.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and repeated. The Markdown does not show automatic execution, so injection risk depends on how future prompts construct command JSON.

dangerous_patterns.title: Real Session Content Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: The bridge can extract page text, HTML-like annotated content, interactable elements, and screenshots from a real browser session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The action reference and workflow both document content extraction. This is a clear privacy-sensitive capability, even without evidence of network exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Browser Process Start
dangerous_patterns.description: The instructions suggest starting Firefox with nohup in the background. This is common troubleshooting, but it creates a persistent local process controlled by the browser bridge.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The nohup command is directly present in quick start and troubleshooting sections. It is not malicious by itself, but persistence is relevant to runtime review.

content.value_statement: Web tasks often require the same logged-in browser state that users already trust. This skill lets Claude, Codex, and Claude Code inspect and operate Firefox through a local bridge.
actual_capabilities: Lists open Firefox tabs and selects the active tab for agent work.
Creates new browser sessions and navigates to requested URLs.
Reads page content in text or annotated formats with interactable elements.
Clicks elements, types text, and fills forms using selectors or visible text.
Captures visible-page screenshots when a filename is provided.
Uses existing browser authentication state after user review and approval.
limitations: Requires Firefox, the bridge, and the browser command to be installed locally.
Can expose private page content from logged-in sessions if used carelessly.
Does not guarantee every website interaction succeeds across dynamic layouts.
Should not be used for irreversible account actions without explicit user approval.

use_cases.title: Authenticated Workflow Testing
use_cases.description: Check a logged-in web application flow using the same Firefox session as the user.

use_cases.title: Research Across Open Tabs
use_cases.description: Inspect existing tabs, read page content, and compare information without copying pages manually.

use_cases.title: Form Completion Assistance
use_cases.description: Fill repetitive web forms with reviewed values while keeping the user in control of submission.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Current Tabs
prompt_templates.prompt: List my open Firefox tabs, identify the active tab, and summarize what you can safely inspect.

prompt_templates.title: Read a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open a new Firefox session for this URL, read the annotated page content, and summarize the main actions available.

prompt_templates.title: Complete a Reviewed Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Firefox to fill this form with the values I provide. Stop before submitting and ask me to confirm.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Sites
prompt_templates.prompt: Create isolated Firefox tabs for these product pages, extract the visible prices and shipping details, and compare the results.

output_examples.input: List my current tabs and tell me which one is active.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the active tab, groups open tabs by window, and highlights pages that may contain private account data.

output_examples.input: Read this logged-in dashboard and find the export button.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the visible dashboard sections and identifies the export control without pressing it.

output_examples.input: Compare prices for the same item on two shopping pages.
output_examples.output: The assistant opens separate sessions, reads visible product details, and returns a concise price comparison.

best_practices: Start with a new session or explicit tab selection before interacting with pages.
Review any action that submits forms, changes settings, or uses authentication.
Use annotated page content before clicks so selectors are chosen from current page state.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the agent to act inside sensitive accounts without clear boundaries.
Do not submit purchases, payments, or account changes without user confirmation.
Do not paste secrets into pages unless the destination and purpose are verified.

faq.question: Does this use my real Firefox profile?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes using the real Firefox session with existing cookies and logins.

faq.question: Can it work with authenticated websites?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use existing browser authentication state, but sensitive actions should require explicit approval.

faq.question: Can it take screenshots?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented screenshot action captures the visible area as a PNG.

faq.question: Is this safe for private accounts?
faq.answer: Use caution. It can read and interact with private pages that are open in Firefox.

faq.question: Does it run without local setup?
faq.answer: No. Firefox and the browser bridge command must be available in the local environment.

faq.question: Should it perform final submissions automatically?
faq.answer: No. Final submissions, purchases, and account changes should wait for direct user confirmation.
